name: Christine's Jamaican Jerk Sauce
recipeIngredient: 4 Scotch Bonnet Peppers (4-6 seeds and all, or equivalent)
1 tablespoon Ground Thyme (or 2 tablespoons fresh thyme leaves)
recipeInstructions:
1. Place all ingredients in a food processor and blend until smooth.
If using whole allspice berries, use enough to equal 1/2 cup ground.
2. The sauce will keep indefinitely in the refrigerator.
3. Feel free to increase the garlic and hot peppers according to preference.
4. Rub about 1/4 cup of sauce into each chicken piece (halved) or pork shoulder cut, ensuring it gets under the skin and in all cavities.
5. For pork, use a deboned shoulder; score the fat and apply the sauce generously, using at least 1/2 cup per 6-pound shoulder.
6. Use less sauce for fish.
7. Marinate overnight if possible and grill over low heat until cooked through.
8. Charcoal is ideal for achieving a smoky flavor.
9. The meat should be a pinkish color when done, and the skin darkened.
10. Serve with hard-dough bread and plenty of Red Stripe Beer.
This Jamaican Jerk Sauce recipe was originally posted by Christine Morin, a restaurant owner, caterer, and chef from Jamaica. It can be made in bulk, refrigerated, and used to marinate chicken (whole, halves, or wings), pork (chops or deboned shoulder, or fresh picnic), or firm-fleshed fish like grouper or dolphin. The sauce features the Scotch Bonnet pepper, a highly flavorful, aromatic, and spicy Jamaican pepper that makes a jalapeno seem mild in comparison. Scotch Bonnets are closely related to peppers found throughout the Caribbean, Central and South America, and even into the West Coast of the US, such as the Habanero.
